The Jubur (Arabic: الجبور) are an Iraqi Arab tribe that forms part of the larger Zubayd tribal confederation, which belongs to the Madhhij group. They trace their lineage to the legendary pre-Islamic warrior Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi (died 642 CE), a cavalry commander who converted to Islam and served in major battles including Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, and Nahavand under the Rashidun Caliphate. Along with the Azza, Dulaim, Janabi and Obaidi federations, the Jubur are sub-groups of the Zubaydi tribe, one of the prominent Arabian tribal groups of Iraq.

A significant portion of the Jubur tribe settled in Hawija and Kirkuk in the eighteenth century, establishing themselves in northern Iraq. The tribe was originally entirely Sunni Muslim until the 19th century, when some members began converting to Shia Islam, particularly in the mid-Euphrates region of southern Iraq. This religious transformation reflects the broader sectarian dynamics that have shaped Iraqi tribal society over the centuries.

During the Armenian genocide of 1915, the Jubur tribe demonstrated notable humanitarian action by sheltering Armenian deportees in the al-Jazira desert, providing refuge to those being deported by the Ottoman Empire. This action illustrates the tribe's historical role in regional humanitarian affairs during times of crisis.

In modern times, the Jubur have played significant roles in Iraqi military and political affairs. Since 2014, the tribe has actively fought against ISIS, participating in operations to retake Tikrit and other Iraqi cities during the War in Iraq (2013–17). Some Jubur members joined the Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF), the Iranian-backed paramilitary umbrella group that became part of the Iraqi Armed Forces. Following the liberation of Qayyarah in 2016, members of the Jubur tribe from Qayyarah and surrounding villages formed the Popular Mobilization Forces' 39th Regiment, nicknamed 'Commandos of al-Jubur' or 'Lions of the Tigris,' which subsequently participated in the Battle of Mosul (2016–2017). Contemporary tribal leaders include Ahmed Abdullah al-Jubouri, who has served as Governor of Salah ad-Din Governorate, and Najim Abdullah al-Jubouri, a senior military officer known for his role as Governor of Nineveh and for leading military operations during the liberation of Mosul from ISIS.

This warrior was renowned as a cavalry commander who converted to Islam and served in major battles including Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, and Nahavand under the Rashidun Caliphate.

data.history: The Khanabil are a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountains and Musandam Peninsula of the UAE and Oman. According to the tribal structure documented in historical sources, the Shihuh divide into two primary sections: the Bani Hadiyah and the Bani Shatair. The Bani Shatair, which includes the Khanabil alongside the Kumazarah, Mahabib, and Maqadilah subsections, are primarily centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula. The Shihuh tribe as a whole incorporates elements of Iranian origin, with the Arab majority believed to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves that brought Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century CE. At the turn of the 20th century, the entire Shihuh tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with 14,500 settled along the coast and 7,000 in the mountainous interior, though tribal members would travel seasonally between both domains. The Khanabil, as part of the Bani Shatair division, participated in the tribe's distinctive seasonal migration patterns. During winter months, they formed agricultural communities in stone-built mountain settlements, channeling water run-off to irrigate stepped fields since no wells existed in the mountains. In warmer months, they migrated to work during the date harvest season, typically to Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman, and also participated in the pearl fishing season. They maintained large herds of goats as their primary source of tradeable surplus. The Shihuh were historically difficult to govern due to their mountainous terrain and frequently conflicted with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, generally preferring Muscat's suzerainty. The Khanabil, along with other Shihuh subsections, played roles in significant regional conflicts including the 1921-1922 battle for Rams, where they allied with local leaders against Al Qasimi authority, and various disputes that shaped the political boundaries of towns like Dibba and Kalba. Their strategic position in the Musandam Peninsula made them influential in the affairs of both the east and west coast settlements of the northern UAE and Oman throughout their history.

data.history: The Kumazarah are a subsection of the Bani Shatair branch of the Shihuh tribe, inhabiting the Musandam Peninsula in northern Oman and the UAE. Unlike the Arab majority of the Shihuh confederation, the Kumazarah are ethnically Iranian (Persian) and speak the Kumzari language, an Iranian language with heavy Arabic, Portuguese, English, and Balochi influences. Bertram Thomas described their dialect as 'a strange tongue which has baffled and confused strangers. It is a compound of Arabic and Persian but it is distinct from them both, and is intelligible neither to the Arab nor to the Persian nor yet to the linguist of both.' The Bani Shatair, including the Kumazarah, are centered around the village of Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula, while the other major Shihuh branch, the Bani Hadiyah, primarily inhabits the western coast including Khasab. The village of Kumzar was historically ruled by a shaikh elected by both the Kumzari and Shihuh people, with many Kumzari shaikhs marrying outside their village. As part of the broader Shihuh confederation, the Kumazarah maintained a semi-nomadic lifestyle, practicing agriculture during winter months in stone-built mountain settlements and migrating seasonally to work during date seasons in places like Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman. They also participated in pearl fishing and maintained large goat herds for trade. The Kumazarah are traditional fishermen and have been regarded as brave fighters. During weddings, Kumzari men perform traditional dances like the Dandana. They regularly travel to the village of Khasab for trade as part of their semi-nomadic traditions.

data.history:
The Mahashir constitute one of the main branches of the powerful Bani Khalid tribal confederation that dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. According to historical sources, the Mahashir were listed among the principal branches of Bani Khalid, which included the Al Humaid, Al Asfour, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and the Mahashir & Nahood. While the chieftainship of the confederation was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan, the Mahashir represented a significant tribal division within this broader structure that controlled the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif oases during the 16th and 17th centuries.

The genealogical origins of the Mahashir, like other Bani Khalid branches, remain disputed among Arab genealogists. Various claims link the confederation to Khalid ibn al-Walid of Banu Makhzum from Quraysh, to Banu Rabi'ah bin Amir bin Sa'sa'a from Hawazin, or to Banu Ghazia from Tayy. Some genealogists question whether they descend from Khalid ibn al-Walid himself or from his relatives, with historical texts noting the possible extinction of Khalid's direct lineage and inheritance passing to his relative Ayoub bin Salama bin Abdullah bin Al-Walid.

Under the leadership of Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid confederation, including the Mahashir, successfully expelled Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabia and established their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The confederation's domain extended across the deserts of al-Hasa and al-Qatif, with the Mahashir participating in the complex tribal politics and economic networks of the Gulf region. Like other Bani Khalid branches, the Mahashir likely adopted Shi'ite Islam over time, creating lasting animosity with the Wahhabi movement and the House of Saud.

The decline of Bani Khalid power began with Saudi invasions starting in 1793, when the Saudis invaded al-Hasa and deposed the ruling Al 'Ura'yir. Following temporary Ottoman restoration after 1818, the confederation faced defeats to tribal alliances including battles with the Mutayr and Ajman tribes in 1823, and final battles with the Subay' and Saudis in 1830 that ended their rule. Many Mahashir members, along with other Bani Khalid branches, subsequently dispersed across the Gulf region, settling in modern-day Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, UAE, Qatar, and Iraq, where their descendants continue to maintain tribal identity within the broader Gulf Arab social structure.

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

data.history:
The Mariandyni were an ancient tribe of Thracian origin located in northeastern Bithynia in ancient Anatolia (modern-day Turkey), not in the Arabian Peninsula. They inhabited the region between the Sangarius and Billaeus Rivers, east of the Thyni and Bithyni tribes. According to ancient sources, their territory was called Mariandynia, with Pliny referencing a 'Sinus Mariandynus' (Mariandynian Gulf) along their coast. The tribe's ethnic origins remain disputed among ancient historians - Strabo believed they were a branch of the Bithynians, while Herodotus distinguished them from other Thracian groups and noted their armor resembled Paphlagonian rather than Bithynian equipment.

The chief city in their territory was Heraclea Pontica, whose inhabitants eventually reduced the Mariandyni to a state of collective serfdom similar to the Cretan Mnoae or Thessalian Penestae. According to modern researcher John Hind, the Mariandyni may have initially ceded coastal territory to Heracleot colonists peacefully, seeking protection from the Bebrykes and Paphlagones. Over time, the Herakleots acquired the Lycus Valley and locked the Mariandyni into agricultural villages as a dependent people, also subject to impressment as fleet rowers.

In the early 6th century BC, they remained independent, paying tribute directly to Lydian king Croesus and warring with Heraclea. Under Persian rule following Cyrus the Great's conquest of Lydia in 546 BC, the Mariandyni formed part of the third Persian satrapy. The Persian Empire included diverse peoples from across its vast territories, with the Mariandyni listed among the various ethnicities that served in Persian armies alongside Persians, Medes, Assyrians, and many other subject peoples.

The tribe is connected to Greek mythology through Mariandynus, their eponymous hero, and figures like Titias, who was said to be either a son of Zeus or the eldest son of Mariandynus. Titias was eventually deified by the Mariandynians and had a city named after him. His sons included Priolaus, Lycus and Mariandynus, with some sources naming Bormus instead of Priolaus. The Mariandyni were known for their mourning songs performed in memory of fallen heroes, particularly after Priolaus was killed in battle against the Bebrycians, which the Mariandyni won with assistance from Heracles.

data.history:
Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Mudar traced descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing it among the foundational ancestors of the 'northern Arabs' (Adnanites) as distinct from the southern Qahtanite tribes. The confederation divided into two major branches through Mudar's sons: Ilyās (also called al-Yās) and ʿAylān (Qays ʿAylān), from whom descended influential tribes including Kināna, Quraysh, Tamīm, Hudhayl, and Asad.

The earliest historical evidence for Mudar appears in South Arabian inscriptions. The al-῾Irāfa 1 inscription (c. 440 CE) from Zafar, capital of Himyar, records a Mudarite chief traveling to Mas'al Jumh to meet the Himyarite king, indicating Mudar was then under Himyarite hegemony. The Maʾsal 2 inscription (521 CE) mentions Mudar providing reinforcements to a Himyarite expedition into Sasanian territory under Banu Tha'labah leadership. During this period, Mudar tribes occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions.

Mudar rose to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, who migrated to Syria. At various times they were under the suzerainty of southern Arabian powers, particularly the Kingdom of Kinda whose rulers bore the title 'king of Maʿadd' or 'king of Mudar and Rabīʿa'. Following the decline of Himyarite authority and collapse of Kindite rule, Mudar tribes emerged as autonomous actors in central Arabia. During the later sixth century, sections of Mudar expanded into northern Arabia and Mesopotamia, where the name Diyār Muḍar came to designate a distinct tribal territory.

With the rise of Islam, many Mudar tribes entered the Muslim polity, though not simultaneously or uniformly. The Quraysh, descended from the Mudarite Kināna branch, became particularly significant as the tribe of Prophet Muhammad. In early Islamic historiography, 'Mudar' increasingly functioned as a political label within the broader tribal dualism opposing northern Arabs (Mudar and Rabīʿa) to southern Yemeni (Qahtanite) tribal groupings, a division that became especially prominent after the first Islamic civil wars. Together with Rabīʿa, Mudar formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd, representing a major political and cultural bloc in Arabian tribal politics.

folkLegends.title: Division of Nizār's Inheritance
folkLegends.story: According to genealogical narratives, when Nizār died, his sons divided their heritage, resulting in Mudar gaining the epithet 'the Red' (al-ḥamrāʿ) because he received his father's red tent while his brothers Rabīʿa, Anmar, and Iyad received other portions of the inheritance.

folkLegends.title: Mudar's Burial and Pilgrimage Site
folkLegends.story: Tradition states that Mudar settled in Mecca and was buried in al-Rawha, where his tomb became a site of pilgrimage in later centuries.

data.history: The Naqbiyin (النقبي, singular Al Naqbi) is a UAE tribe that derives its name from the Wadi Naqab area of Ras Al Khaimah, where they were originally concentrated. The tribe dispersed from this agriculturally rich wadi after disputes with pre-Al Qawasim local rulers, likely between 1695-1740, with many members relocating to settlements in Sharjah emirate including Dibba, Khor Fakkan, and Kalba. By around 1800, their former territory in Wadi Naqab had been acquired by the Habus tribe. The Naqbiyin established strategic settlements throughout the region, including Khatt, Daftah in Wadi Ham, and Fahlain on the Jiri plain. In 1903, Lorimer documented Fahlain as consisting of 60 Naqbiyin houses with 2,000 date palms, primarily engaged in agriculture. The tribe maintained close relations with the Qawasim rulers, with their settlements in Khatt and Fahlain forming part of Hassan bin Rahma's sheikhdom when he signed the 1820 General Maritime Treaty with the British. On the east coast (Shamaliyah), the Naqbiyin were frequently in conflict with the neighboring Sharqiyin tribe but played influential roles in tribal politics. They were considered the 'powers behind the throne' when Kalba gained independence from Sharjah in 1937 under Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi. The Al Qasimi rulers strategically granted them lands as protection against the Sharqiyin, recognizing their presence in Khor Fakkan even before the Portuguese sack of 1506. The tribe's settlement in Khatt reportedly occurred over a 300-year period, and the Al Naqbi Tower still stands in the village today. By the early 20th century, their total settled population across the region numbered approximately 1,800 people. The Naqbiyin were also present in the Wadi Madha area alongside the Madhahana, Bani Hamid, Bani Sa'ad, and Dahwahir tribes.

data.history:
The Qubaisat (Al Kubaisat/Al Qubaisi) are documented as a section of the Bani Yas tribe with a complex history of secession and territorial disputes in the 19th century Gulf region. Historical records show that members of this tribal section migrated to and settled at Khor Al Adaid on three separate occasions during the 19th century: 1835, 1849, and 1869. The first major secession occurred in 1836 when Al-Kubaisat, under Sheikh Khadim-bin-Nahman, sought to avoid consequences of certain piratical activities and established themselves at Khor Al Adaid, breaking allegiance with Abu Dhabi. This initial settlement was violently suppressed in 1837 when the Sheikh of Abu Dhabi, with British approval, sent troops that killed 50 inhabitants and dismantled the settlement's houses and fortifications.

The most significant and lasting secession occurred in 1869 under Sheikh Buttye-bin-Khadim, when the Qubaisat again left Abu Dhabi to resettle at Khor Al Adaid. This colony grew to approximately 200 Bani Yas tribespeople who owned 30 pearling ships and built defensive fortifications including a small fort with two towers. Sheikh Buttye-bin-Khadim asserted territorial claims extending from Ras-al-Hala to Sir Bani Yas island, declaring this territory as 'the ancient home of the Al-Kubaisat.' The settlement became entangled in Ottoman-British imperial rivalry when Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani offered the Ottoman flag to the Qubaisat, though Sheikh Buttye-bin-Khadim initially refused, claiming to be under British protection while occasionally hoisting the Ottoman flag on Fridays.

The Khor Al Adaid settlement became a focal point of regional conflict and diplomatic tension throughout the 1870s. The area was used as a base for piratical activities, though British investigations determined that while pirates operated from Qubaisat ports, the tribal leadership was too weak to prevent this rather than actively supporting piracy. Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi repeatedly requested British permission to reassert authority over the settlement, leading to unsuccessful reconciliation attempts between the two sheikhs. The situation deteriorated when continued piracy and the refusal to release captured sailors from Al Wakrah prompted British support for Abu Dhabi's claims.

The final destruction of the Qubaisat settlement at Khor Al Adaid came in 1879 when Sheikh Zayed, supported by British naval forces with 70 boats and accompanied by an English war steamer, launched a massive invasion that resulted in the village being pillaged and destroyed, with all boats carried off and inhabitants fled. This military action created significant Ottoman diplomatic protest, as they claimed Khor Al Adaid as Turkish territory. Following this destruction, attempts were made by Sheikh Jassim to resettle members of the Kubaisat tribe from Al Wakrah back to Khor Al Adaid in 1891, with about 40 tribal members expressing willingness to return provided one of Jassim's sons served as their sheikh, though these resettlement plans were ultimately abandoned. The territorial dispute over Khor Al Adaid contributed to broader regional conflicts and remained a point of contention between Qatar, Abu Dhabi, and later the UAE well into the modern era.

data.history: The Rubatab people (Arabic: الرباطاب, ar-rubāṭāb) are a Sudanese Arab tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile River in northern Sudan, specifically within the Abu Hamad District stretching north of Berber until Abu Hamad. With a population of approximately 60,000 as of 2012, they constitute a sub-group of the larger Ja'alin tribal confederation, one of the three prominent Sudanese Arab tribes in northern Sudan. The Rubatab maintain their tribal homeland in what is described as poor and barren rocky lands south of Abu Hamad, bordering the Ababda people, Bishari tribe, and Manasir tribe. Like their neighboring riverine tribes including the mid-stream Manasir and downstream Shaiqiyah, the Rubatab are classified as an Arab tribe of Northeastern Sudan who speak an archaic form of Arabic as their mother tongue. However, scholars have classified the Ja'alin confederation, including the Rubatab, as 'Afro-Arab hybrid' peoples representing a mixture of indigenous Africans and Arabs who came to Sudan between the 9th and 14th centuries. Earlier Western scholars described this ethnic composition using various terms including 'bastardized Arab blood, paganized Islam, and creolized Arabic.' The tribe maintains traditional riverine culture along the Nile with documented customs from 1918 including specific inheritance practices where property is shared among heirs and each family maintains genealogical records. Their social structure includes distinctive marriage customs where girls typically marry paternal cousins, elaborate wedding ceremonies featuring communal feasts with dancing and the singing of praise songs called Sumar, and complex dowry arrangements involving both land (mahr) and money (tu'ma). Religious practices blend Islamic beliefs with local traditions including veneration of various walis (saints) and belief in houris described as white-skinned beings with long flowing hair who live in underwater Nile villages.

folkLegends.title: The Five Brothers and the Division of Land
folkLegends.story: According to oral tradition, all the land between Khartoum and Dongola was once owned by a sheikh who had five sons. Before dying, he pleaded with them to avoid quarrels. When dividing their inheritance, the eldest son Robat called his brothers together and said 'I am the eldest and I will set you an example that we may all live together in peace as our father wished. I will take first choice and will choose the rocky lands south of Abu Hamed.' This explains why the Rubatab occupy what is considered the worst, most barren land in the district.

data.nameEtymology: Named after their ancestor Robat (also spelled Rabat), who according to local legend was the eldest son in the folk tale of the five brothers' land division

timelineEvents.title: Documentation of Tribal Customs
timelineEvents.description: British administrator and archaeologist John Winter Crowfoot published detailed documentation of Rubatab history and customs in Sudan Notes and Records, based on descriptions from a Rubatab sheikh

data.history: Sanhan is a Qahtani Arab tribe with deep historical roots in the highlands southeast of Sanaa, Yemen. Ancient Sabaean inscriptions dating to the 7th century BCE consistently mention Sanhan alongside Khawlan, indicating their longstanding presence in the region. The research indicates that Sanhan maintained close relationships with neighboring Khawlan tribes and was part of broader Qahtani genealogical networks. Ancient Sabaean texts reference military campaigns in Wadi Dafa where Sanhan resided alongside Dadan tribes, and this location remains inhabited by Sanhan today. The tribe gained significant political prominence through Ali Abdullah Saleh, who was born in 1947 in Beit al-Ahmar village in Sanhan territory, approximately 20 kilometers southeast of Sanaa. Despite not being from a traditionally powerful sheikhly family, Saleh rose through military ranks after joining the Royal Armed Forces in 1958 and participating in the 1962 Nasserist coup that established the Yemen Arab Republic. After becoming president in 1978 following Ahmad al-Ghashmi's assassination, Saleh created an extensive patronage system centered on his Sanhan tribe. Approximately 70% of command positions in Yemen's security services and army were filled by Sanhan members, making the tribe the backbone of his three-decade rule. The tribe's political influence extended through Saleh's extended family network, with his brothers, sons, and nephews occupying key government positions. Even after Saleh's death in 2017 during fighting with Houthi forces in Sanaa, Sanhan remains an influential tribal group in Yemen's complex political landscape.

data.history: The Shwaihiyin are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, documented as one of the Arab tribes with presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. According to early 20th century records, the Shwaihiyin were one of the two remaining nomadic sections of the Bani Kaab, alongside the Drisah, while other tribal sections including the Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Yidwah and Zahairat had settled by the turn of the 20th century. The tribe's traditional territories were associated with areas around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The Shwaihiyin became prominently involved in Gulf politics during the mid-19th century when a blood feud erupted between them and the Huwalah tribe in Sharjah in 1855. At this time, they were described as recent immigrants to Sharjah, numbering approximately 500 fighting men. Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi relocated the Shwaihiyin from Sharjah to Hamriyah to resolve the conflict, but this move triggered the first rebellion by Hamriyah against Al Qasimi rule. The resulting siege of Hamriyah in May 1855 saw fierce fighting, with the Shwaihiyin and local defenders holding out against a combined force from Sharjah and Ajman. British mediation eventually resolved the conflict, resulting in the Shwaihiyin being removed from Hamriyah. Like other sections of the Bani Kaab, the Shwaihiyin were involved in the complex tribal politics of southeastern Arabia. As part of the broader Bani Kaab confederation, they participated in the shifting alliances that characterized the region, including the 1844 alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan against Wahhabi forces, and later alignment with Saudi Arabia in the mid-20th century. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab, including the Shwaihiyin, supported the Saudi incursion and were involved in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

data.history: The Banū Tamīm (Arabic: بَنُو تَمِيم) are one of the largest and most prominent Adnanite Arab tribes, tracing their lineage through Tamīm ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan to the biblical Ishmael. The tribe originated in Najd and the Hejaz regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with the word Tamim meaning 'strong and solid' in Arabic. During the pre-Islamic period, they occupied numerous wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century. The Banu Tamim are traditionally divided into four main branches: Amr, Banu Hanzala, Banu Sa'd, and Al-Ribbab (Al-Rabbab). The tribe came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. A hadith records Muhammad's praise: 'They will be the sternest of my Ummah against the Dajjal.' After the Islamic conquests, they spread extensively across the caliphates, establishing significant power in various regions. They founded the Aghlabid dynasty which ruled Ifriqiya (Tunisia, Algeria, and Libya) from 800-909 CE. The Al Thani ruling family of Qatar, from the Banu Tamim, moved from the Najdi town of Ushaiger to the Gebrin oasis in southern Najd before settling in Qatar around the 1720s, first in Sikak, then Zubarah, and finally Doha in the 19th century under Mohammed bin Thani. The tribe produced notable poets like al-Farazdaq and Jarir, military commanders like al-Qa'qa'a ibn Amr during the Rashidun conquests, and religious scholars including Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founder of the Nejdi Salafi movement. Today, Tamim descendants are found throughout Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, Kuwait, Iraq, Oman, Jordan, Lebanon, and across North Africa including Algeria, Morocco, Tunisia, and Libya, as well as in Iran's Khuzestan province.

folkLegends.title: Meeting with Jesus
folkLegends.story: According to the Zaydi Shi'a scholar Al-Mansur Abdullah, Tamim ibn Murr met with Jesus and became an adherent to Christianity. Other narrations say that Tamim was one of the Disciples of Jesus.

folkLegends.title: Descent from Abraham
folkLegends.story: In the genealogical tradition of the tribe, there is a direct line drawn from Abraham through Ishmael, Adnan, Ma'add, Nizar, Mudar, Ilyas, Amr (Tabikhah), 'Ud, Murr, to Tamim.

data.nameEtymology: The word Tamim in Arabic means 'strong and solid.' It can also mean those who strive for perfection. The name is derived from the Arabic root meaning firmness and strength.

data.history:
The Zahran tribe traces its lineage to Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd, making it an Azdite offshoot of Qahtanite origin. The name Zahran, meaning 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic, refers to their shared common ancestor. The tribe's homeland is centered in Al-Bahah Province, particularly the Sarawat Mountains region, which they share with the neighboring Ghamid tribe. The Zahran are part of the larger Azd Shanu'ah tribal confederation, characterized by their light, muscular build and eloquent poetry traditions.

In the 3rd century CE, many Zahranid branches migrated under the leadership of Malik ibn Fehm, who became one of the first Arabians to settle in Oman. This migration led to conflict with Persian forces in the great Salut Battle, contributing to Oman's Arabization. Malik ibn Fehm later established the Tanukh tribal confederation centered in Al-Hira, with his son Jadhima 'the Leper' becoming king of Tanukh in the second half of the 3rd century CE. The Al-Jadara family, descended from Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama, settled in Mecca and became known as 'the Wall-Masons' for reconstructing the Kaaba's walls after Abraham and Ishmael. They formed crucial alliances with Banu Al Du'al and Quraysh, with Fatimah bint Sa'd Al-Jadari marrying Kilab ibn Murrah of Quraysh, bearing Qusay ibn Kilab, an ancestor of Prophet Muhammad.

The tribe was well-known before and after Islam, with many members joining Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The tribe is notable for producing Abu Hurairah, one of Islam's most prolific hadith narrators from the Banu Daws clan of Zahran. Abu Hurairah converted to Islam through Tufayl ibn 'Amr in 629 CE and became one of the inhabitants of the Suffah, participating in various military expeditions and later serving as governor of Bahrain under Caliph Umar.

The Zahran tribe comprises three main divisions: Banu Daws (including Banu Manhib, Banu Fahm and Banu Ali), Banu 'Amr (including Banu Bashir, Banu Harir, Banu Jundob, and Banu 'Adwan), and Banu Aws (including Banu Hasan, Bal-Khirmar, Banu Kinanah, Banu 'Amir and Ahl Baydan). Modern demographic changes occurred in the 1960s and 1970s when many Zahranis migrated to major Saudi cities like Riyadh, Jeddah, Mecca, and Dammam seeking better opportunities. Oman's current ruling family, Al Said, claims descent from Zahran through Malik ibn Fehm, while the tribe maintains its traditional homeland in Al-Bahah while having established significant presences across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

folkLegends.title: The Wall-Masons of Mecca
folkLegends.story: Al-Jadara were a Zahranid family that settled in Mecca and were called 'Al-Jadra' (the Wall-Masons) because their progenitor, Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama, is said to be the first to reconstruct the walls of the Kaaba after Abraham and Ishmael, earning him the title 'Amer the Builder'.

folkLegends.title: The Destruction of Dhu l-Khalasah
folkLegends.story: Dhu l-Khalasah was an idol worshiped by the Daws clan in a valley in Zahran's homeland. In the early 20th century, local clans returned to honoring this idol during times of security crisis and poverty. King Abdulaziz (Ibn Saud) ordered its destruction between 1921-1925 CE, carried out by Abdulaziz Al Ibrahim who led a campaign that demolished the structure and threw its ruins into a nearby valley.

data.nameEtymology: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'.

data.history:
The Banu Kinda, also known as simply Kinda or Kindah (Arabic: كِنْدَة), was one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederations, originating from South Arabia in the region west of Hadramawt as early as the 3rd century CE. The tribe's genealogy traced back through Kinda ibn Afsa (nicknamed Thawr ibn Ufayr) to the semi-legendary Kahlan, making them ethnically distinct from non-Arab South Arabians like the Himyar. The Kinda consisted of three principal branches: the Banu Mu'awiya (the leading branch), the Sakun, and the Sakasik, with the latter two often grouped together as the Ashras. From the Banu Mu'awiya descended the royal households, including the Banu Akil al-Murar who would rule in central Arabia, and the Banu Wali'a in Hadramawt.

Initially serving as Bedouin auxiliaries to the Sabaean Kingdom, the Kinda later allied with the Himyarite Kingdom under the Jewish king Dhu Nuwas in the early 6th century. In the mid-5th century, part of the tribe migrated northward with Himyarite support and established the Kingdom of Kinda over the Ma'add tribal confederation in central and northern Arabia. This kingdom was founded by Hujr, who established the Banu Akil al-Murar royal household, and reached its zenith under his grandson al-Harith ibn Amr. Al-Harith captured the Lakhmid capital of al-Hira and became a Byzantine federate, but the kingdom began to fragment when he divided rule among his four sons - Hujr, Ma'dikarib, Shurahbil, and Salama.

Internal strife among al-Harith's sons led to the kingdom's collapse by the late 6th century. The brothers became absorbed in their constituents' blood feuds, with several Kindite kings being killed in internecine fighting. The decisive blow came with defeats at battles including Shi'b Jabala (circa 550-580 CE), which forced approximately 30,000 Kindites to abandon their settlements in Najd and Yamama and return to Hadramawt. The Kindite migration included departures from their major settlements of Ghamr Dhi Kinda in Najd and Hajar and al-Mushaqqar in the Yamama.

The Kinda accepted Islam during Muhammad's lifetime, with leaders like the Banu Wali'a and al-Ash'ath ibn Qays sending deputations to the Prophet. Muhammad granted the Wali'a a designated portion of tax revenue from Hadramawt. However, the tribe rebelled during the Ridda Wars (632-633 CE) when the Muslim governor Ziyad ibn Labid al-Ansari withheld their designated tax revenues. Al-Ash'ath emerged as the most powerful Kindite chief during this period, leading resistance from the fortress of Nujayr before ultimately being defeated and pardoned. After the Ridda Wars, surviving Kindite leaders like al-Ash'ath played important roles in the early Muslim conquests and held prominent positions throughout the Caliphate, serving as governors and military commanders in Iraq, Syria, Armenia, and Azerbaijan.

folkLegends.title: The Founding of Kindite Rule over Ma'add
folkLegends.story: According to medieval Arabic literary sources, the subordination of the nomadic tribes of Ma'add to Kindite rule was initiated by the Ma'add themselves, especially the Bakr ibn Wa'il division, to bring order to constantly feuding constituent tribes. The Bakr sent envoys to the king of Himyar, inviting him to be their king. Instead, the king delegated the role to Hujr for unclear reasons, leading to the establishment of Kindite monarchy over central Arabia.
folkLegends.source: medieval Arabic literary works of al-Isfahani and Ibn Habib
